one_line_takeaway: Graph-structured deep learning models trained directly on stripped-down x86-64 opcode/ModR/M/SIB byte sequences (no disassembly, no operand bytes) reach 76-93% accuracy on three CWE classes from the FormAI-v2 dataset, with CFG-aware GCN models beating flat sequential CNN models by a wide margin, suggesting machine code alone carries enough signal for vulnerability detection without assembly-level semantics -- though this is shown via an uncontrolled cross-dataset comparison to prior work, not a same-dataset baseline.
key_finding: Graph-based (GCN/CFG) models consistently and substantially outperformed sequential (1D-CNN-only) models across all three vulnerability types. Best sequential results used filter size k=7: Null Pointer Dereference accuracy 0.8152/F1 0.8145; Array Bound Violation accuracy 0.7584/F1 0.7617; Integer Overflow accuracy 0.7334/F1 0.7339 (Table IV). Combining filter sizes {3,5,7} did not improve over the best single size. Best graph-based results: a 2-layer GCN gave the best Null Pointer Dereference (accuracy 0.9323/F1 0.9332) and Array Bound Violation (accuracy 0.8661/F1 0.8654) performance, while a 1-layer GCN was best for Integer Overflow (accuracy 0.8392/F1 0.8389) (Table V); performance degraded at 3 GCN layers (attributed to possible over-smoothing) before partially recovering at 4 layers. Across all configurations, Null Pointer Dereference classifiers performed best and Integer Overflow classifiers performed worst, which the authors attribute to integer overflows depending on arithmetic/data-flow properties not well captured by control-flow-only representations. The paper's own headline claim is that these machine-code-only results are comparable to Bin2Vec's reported 80-90% accuracy across CWE classes on the (different) Juliet dataset, supporting the feasibility claim that raw machine code retains enough signal for vulnerability detection without assembly-level semantic richness -- but this is a cross-dataset, non-controlled comparison, not a head-to-head experiment.
core_contribution: An exploratory study on whether deep learning vulnerability detectors can be trained directly on raw x86-64 machine code (opcode/prefix/ModR/M/SIB bytes, with displacement and immediate operand bytes stripped to bound vocabulary size), rather than on disassembled assembly text or intermediate representations as prior work (e.g., Bin2Vec, Asm2Vec, PalmTree) does. The paper builds two model families over this machine-code tokenization: (1) a sequential architecture (instruction embedding -> 1D-CNN over instruction sequences -> multi-head self-attention over functions -> feedforward classifier) and (2) a graph-based architecture that additionally represents each function's control flow graph (CFG) as a feature matrix of per-basic-block instruction sequences plus an adjacency matrix, processed with a GCN and top-K attention pooling before the same self-attention/feedforward aggregation. Both are trained as binary (vulnerable/non-vulnerable) classifiers, one model per vulnerability class.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the security-research sense; this is a detection/classification task rather than an attack or defense-under-adversary setup. The 'threat' addressed is the presence of exploitable memory-safety/arithmetic bugs (pointer misuse, buffer/array bound errors, integer overflow) in compiled binaries where source code or disassembly may be unavailable, imprecise, or costly to obtain -- the paper frames binary-only analysis as necessary when vendors withhold source or when disassembly introduces heuristic inaccuracies/information loss. No adversarial evasion, obfuscation-robustness, or attacker-capability model is evaluated in this paper (obfuscation robustness is mentioned only as motivation for related work like Asm2Vec, not tested here).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) only three vulnerability types studied (CWE-476, CWE-129, CWE-190), excluding e.g. race conditions and use-after-free; (2) dataset diversity is limited to FormAI-v2's Linux-based, LLM-generated C code, so generalization to other OSes/binary formats (PE/Windows vs ELF/Linux) and real-world (non-LLM-generated) codebases is untested; (3) the models rely on control-flow relationships and local instruction context only, not data-flow analysis, which the authors link directly to the weak Integer Overflow results; (4) no interpretability beyond raw self-attention scores -- the paper does not evaluate whether attention actually localizes vulnerable code; (5) single architecture (x86-64) only, no evaluation on ARM, RISC, or bytecode targets; (6) static CFG construction cannot represent dynamically computed jump targets/return addresses, an inherent limitation of static analysis noted by the authors. Additional grounding-level caveats not flagged by the authors: FormAI-v2 vulnerability labels come entirely from a bounded model checker (ESBMC) rather than real-world CVE/exploit ground truth, so results reflect detection of formally-verified synthetic-LLM-code flaws, not necessarily real-world vulnerability distributions; the Bin2Vec comparison is against a different dataset (Juliet) and different CWE set, so 'comparable performance' is a qualitative, not controlled, claim; and no comparison is made against a modern assembly/IR-based baseline trained on the same FormAI-v2 dataset, so the paper cannot isolate how much of the accuracy is attributable to the machine-code representation choice versus dataset/architecture differences.

datasets_used: FormAI-v2 (331,000 LLM-generated C programs from 9 different LLMs, ~62% labeled vulnerable via ESBMC bounded model checking, deduplicated with NiCad clone detector)
quantitative_results: Sequential model (k=7 CNN filter, best config): Null Pointer Dereference accuracy 0.8152 / F1 0.8145
Sequential model (k=7): Array Bound Violation accuracy 0.7584 / F1 0.7617
Sequential model (k=7): Integer Overflow accuracy 0.7334 / F1 0.7339
Graph model (2-layer GCN, best config): Null Pointer Dereference accuracy 0.9323 / F1 0.9332
Graph model (2-layer GCN): Array Bound Violation accuracy 0.8661 / F1 0.8654
Graph model (1-layer GCN, best config): Integer Overflow accuracy 0.8392 / F1 0.8389
3-layer GCN performance drop across all classes (e.g. Null Pointer Dereference accuracy falls to 0.7647 from 0.9323 at 2 layers), attributed to possible over-smoothing

Dataset scale: 100,436 / 25,068 / 39,996 source files for Null Pointer / Array Bound / Integer Overflow classes respectively, expanded via multi-optimization-flag recompilation and downsampled to 150,408 binaries per class
baselines_compared: Bin2Vec (cited prior work; reported 80-90% accuracy across ~30 CWE classes on the Juliet Test Suite) -- referenced as a qualitative feasibility comparison, not re-run on the same dataset/split as this paper's own models
Asm2Vec and PalmTree are discussed in Related Work as prior assembly-embedding approaches but are not empirically compared against in this paper's experiments
No same-dataset assembly/IR-based baseline is trained or evaluated for direct comparison; the comparison against prior work is entirely across different datasets (FormAI-v2 vs Juliet)
